title: SEGMENT 11 — The 2023 human ETX embryos: gastrulation in a dish

lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — The 2023 human ETX embryos: gastrulation in a dish
Now we come to the most recent — and possibly most consequential — developments in the field.

In 2023, multiple labs published papers showing that the recipe for mouse synthetic embryos could be extended to humans, with human cells, producing structures that developed past the blastocyst stage and into the early post-implantation period — through the start of gastrulation, with recognizable early body axes and beginnings of organogenesis-stage events.
Two papers stand out, both published in June and September 2023.
The first was from Magdalena Zernicka-Goetz's group at the University of Cambridge and Caltech.
The second, again, was from Jacob Hanna's group at the Weizmann Institute.
The two groups had been working in parallel on the same problem, with somewhat different approaches, and they reported converging results within a few months of each other.
The Hanna paper, published in Nature in September 2023, used naive human pluripotent stem cells as the starting point.

Through a sequence of culture conditions analogous to the mouse synthetic embryo protocol, the cells were pushed to generate all three early lineages — epiblast, trophoblast, and hypoblast — in a coordinated way.
They were then allowed to self-organize in a defined gel and culture environment.

By the equivalent of day 14 of human development — the same time when the primitive streak first appears in real embryos, the same time when the original 14-day rule says you have to stop — the synthetic structures had:

A recognizable epiblast disc A surrounding hypoblast layer Trophoblast outer cells with appropriate subtypes A pro-amniotic cavity Early signs of axis polarization The beginnings of primitive-streak-like organization in some specimens
These are not full embryos at this stage.
They're early post-implantation models — at roughly the same developmental level as a real human embryo around day 12 to 14.
They don't have hearts.
They don't have brains.

The mouse synthetic embryos at day 8.5 are much more advanced than the human synthetic embryos at day 14, because the developmental timing differs — humans are slower, and the technical hurdles for pushing human cultures further are still being worked out.
The Zernicka-Goetz paper, posted as a preprint in June 2023 and ultimately published in late 2023, reported a similar achievement.
Human stem cells, combined in the right ratios with appropriate signaling, formed structures with the three lineages, with appropriate axis specification, with early gastrulation-like organization.

The protocols differed in details, the structures had different efficiencies and slightly different morphological features, but the underlying message was the same: the recipe works in humans, at least up to the early post-implantation period, at least at proof-of-concept levels.
A few things to note.
This was done under careful oversight.
Both labs operated under their institutional ethics frameworks, which by 2023 had been updated in light of the 2021 ISSCR guidelines.
The work was reviewed by specialized embryo research oversight committees.
The constructs were not transferred to any human uterus and were not intended to be — that would be prohibited in essentially every jurisdiction and is firmly excluded by ISSCR guidelines.
The work was bench science, with carefully specified developmental endpoints.
The structures vary in fidelity.
As with all synthetic embryo work, the human ETX models are imperfect copies of real embryos.
Some lineages are over- or underrepresented.
The proportions can be off.
Single-cell sequencing has shown subtle differences from real embryos at comparable stages.
The work clearly demonstrates the principle — humans can be modeled this way — but it doesn't claim to produce indistinguishable copies of real embryos.
Efficiency remains low.
Many starting aggregates fail to develop into proper structures.
The papers report yields in the percent or low-tens-of-percent range, depending on protocol details and criteria.
Most starting material doesn't become a proper model.
The successful ones are selected morphologically for downstream analysis.
The structures cannot continue developing indefinitely.
As of 2023, no human synthetic embryo had been pushed to anything close to the heart-and-brain stage that mouse work reaches at day 8.5.

The technical challenges for extending human cultures are significant, both in terms of culture conditions and in terms of ethics — the further you push, the more the ethical scrutiny intensifies, and the more uncertain the regulatory status becomes.
The 2023 human ETX work is, in my view, the most consequential development since the 2021 blastoid papers.

It demonstrates that the synthetic-embryo project — the project of modeling early human development in dishes, from stem cells — is technically achievable, not just for the blastocyst stage but for the post-implantation stages that have historically been inaccessible.
The window into the black box is opening.
It also brings the ethical questions into very sharp focus.

If a synthetic structure recapitulates the events of the second and third weeks of human development — implantation, gastrulation, the early body plan — then we're at exactly the developmental window that the 14-day rule was designed to limit.
The fact that the structure was made from stem cells, not from fertilization, doesn't make the developmental events themselves less morally significant.

Or, depending on your view, it makes them somewhat less morally significant — because the structure wasn't initiated by a conception event, because it isn't traceable to any potential person — but how much less is exactly the question the field is debating.
The 2023 papers were generally well-received in the scientific community as careful, rigorous, important work.
They were generally received with concern in the bioethics community as a development that pushes the field into territory where the ethical guidelines haven't fully caught up.
Both reactions are appropriate.
The work is good science.
The implications are unsettled.
We're now at the point where the technical and the ethical have to be discussed together — you can't have a sensible conversation about one without the other.
That's where the field is.
That's where we'll spend the rest of the lecture.
